notes.heading: Introduction to Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloalkanes and haloarenes are organic compounds formed when one or more hydrogen atoms in hydrocarbons are replaced by halogen atoms such as F, Cl, Br or I.
points: Haloalkanes are organic compounds in which one or more hydrogen atoms of an alkane are replaced by halogen atoms.
General formula of haloalkanes: R-X, where R is an alkyl group and X is a halogen atom.
Examples of haloalkanes: CH3Cl (methyl chloride), C2H5Br (ethyl bromide), CH3CH2CH2Cl (1-chloropropane).
Haloalkanes are also called alkyl halides.
Haloarenes are organic compounds in which one or more hydrogen atoms of an aromatic ring are replaced by halogen atoms.
General formula of haloarenes: Ar-X, where Ar is an aryl group and X is a halogen atom.
Examples of haloarenes: C6H5Cl (chlorobenzene), C6H5Br (bromobenzene).
Haloarenes are also called aryl halides.
In haloalkanes, the halogen is attached to an alkyl group, while in haloarenes, the halogen is attached directly to an aromatic ring.
The C-X bond in haloarenes is generally stronger than the C-X bond in haloalkanes.
Haloalkanes are generally more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than haloarenes.
Haloalkanes and haloarenes are used in medicines, solvents, refrigerants, pesticides and organic synthesis.
Examples of important halogen compounds include chloroform, carbon tetrachloride and DDT.

notes.heading: Classification of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloalkanes and haloarenes are classified on the basis of the number of halogen atoms, the type of carbon attached to halogen and the hybridisation of the carbon attached to halogen.
points: Monohalogen compounds contain one halogen atom. Examples: CH3Cl and C6H5Cl.
Dihalogen compounds contain two halogen atoms. Examples: CH2Cl2 and CHCl2CH3.
Trihalogen compounds contain three halogen atoms. Example: CHCl3.
Tetrahalogen compounds contain four halogen atoms. Example: CCl4.
Primary haloalkanes contain a carbon attached to halogen that is further attached to only one other carbon atom. Example: CH3CH2Cl.
Secondary haloalkanes contain a carbon attached to halogen that is further attached to two other carbon atoms. Example: CH3CHClCH3.
Tertiary haloalkanes contain a carbon attached to halogen that is further attached to three other carbon atoms. Example: (CH3)3CCl.
Compounds containing sp3 C-X bond include alkyl halides, allylic halides and benzylic halides.
Alkyl halides have halogen attached to an alkyl group. Example: CH3CH2Br.
Allylic halides have halogen attached to an sp3 carbon next to a carbon-carbon double bond. Example: CH2=CH-CH2Cl.
Benzylic halides have halogen attached to an sp3 carbon next to a benzene ring. Example: C6H5CH2Cl.
Compounds containing sp2 C-X bond include vinylic halides and aryl halides.
Vinylic halides have halogen attached directly to a carbon-carbon double bond. Example: CH2=CHCl.
Aryl halides have halogen attached directly to an aromatic ring. Example: C6H5Cl.
Benzyl chloride, C6H5CH2Cl, is a benzylic halide because chlorine is attached to the side-chain carbon.
Chlorobenzene, C6H5Cl, is an aryl halide because chlorine is attached directly to the benzene ring.

notes.heading: Nomenclature of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloalkanes and haloarenes are named using common names and IUPAC names. In IUPAC names, halogens are treated as prefixes.
points: In common names of haloalkanes, the compound is named as alkyl group name plus halide.
Examples of common names: CH3Cl is methyl chloride, CH3CH2Br is ethyl bromide, CH3CH2CH2Cl is n-propyl chloride.
In IUPAC names, halogens are written as prefixes: fluoro, chloro, bromo and iodo.
The parent hydrocarbon chain is selected and numbered so that the halogen gets the lowest possible number.
CH3Cl is named chloromethane.
CH3CH2Br is named bromoethane.
CH3CH2CH2Cl is named 1-chloropropane.
CH3CHClCH3 is named 2-chloropropane.
C6H5Cl is called chlorobenzene in both common and IUPAC systems.
C6H5Br is called bromobenzene.
In disubstituted benzene derivatives, positions are represented by ortho, meta and para.
Ortho or o- indicates 1,2-position.
Meta or m- indicates 1,3-position.
Para or p- indicates 1,4-position.
o-dichlorobenzene is 1,2-dichlorobenzene.
m-dichlorobenzene is 1,3-dichlorobenzene.
p-dichlorobenzene is 1,4-dichlorobenzene.
CH2=CHCH2Cl is commonly called allyl chloride and its IUPAC name is 3-chloroprop-1-ene.
C6H5CH2Cl is commonly called benzyl chloride and its IUPAC name is chloromethylbenzene.
CH2=CHCl is commonly called vinyl chloride and its IUPAC name is chloroethene.
If more than one same halogen is present, prefixes such as di, tri and tetra are used. Example: CH2Cl2 is dichloromethane.

notes.heading: Nature of C-X Bond in Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: The carbon-halogen bond is polar because halogens are more electronegative than carbon. This polarity controls many chemical reactions of haloalkanes and haloarenes.
points: The C-X bond is polar because halogen attracts the shared electron pair more strongly than carbon.
The bond polarity can be represented as Cδ+-Xδ-.
In chloromethane, the bond is represented as CH3δ+-Clδ-.
The partially positive carbon atom can be attacked by nucleophiles such as OH-, CN- and NH3.
Example of nucleophilic substitution: CH3Br + OH- → CH3OH + Br-.
As the size of halogen increases, C-X bond length increases.
Bond length order: C-F < C-Cl < C-Br < C-I.
Bond strength decreases as bond length increases.
Bond strength order: C-F > C-Cl > C-Br > C-I.
The usual reactivity order of haloalkanes is RI > RBr > RCl > RF.
Iodoalkanes are most reactive because the C-I bond is weakest.
Fluoroalkanes are least reactive because the C-F bond is strongest.
In haloarenes such as chlorobenzene, the C-X bond is stronger than in haloalkanes.
In chlorobenzene, the lone pair of chlorine overlaps with the benzene ring and gives the C-Cl bond partial double bond character.
Due to partial double bond character, the C-X bond in haloarenes becomes shorter and stronger.
Haloarenes are less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution because the C-X bond is stronger and the benzene ring does not easily allow nucleophilic attack.

notes.heading: Preparation of Haloalkanes from Alcohols
notes.text: Haloalkanes can be prepared from alcohols by replacing the -OH group with a halogen atom using hydrogen halides, phosphorus halides or thionyl chloride.
points: Alcohols contain the -OH group, while haloalkanes contain the -X group.
General conversion: R-OH → R-X.
Alcohols react with hydrogen halides such as HCl, HBr and HI to form haloalkanes.
General reaction with hydrogen halides: R-OH + HX → R-X + H2O.
Example: C2H5OH + HBr → C2H5Br + H2O.
Reactivity order of hydrogen halides: HI > HBr > HCl.
The H-I bond is weakest, so HI reacts fastest.
Reactivity order of alcohols towards hydrogen halides: 3° > 2° > 1°.
Tertiary alcohols react fastest because they form more stable carbocations.
Alcohols react with phosphorus pentachloride to give chloroalkanes: R-OH + PCl5 → R-Cl + POCl3 + HCl.
Example: C2H5OH + PCl5 → C2H5Cl + POCl3 + HCl.
Alcohols react with phosphorus trichloride to form chloroalkanes: 3R-OH + PCl3 → 3R-Cl + H3PO3.
Example: 3C2H5OH + PCl3 → 3C2H5Cl + H3PO3.
Alcohols react with thionyl chloride to form chloroalkanes: R-OH + SOCl2 → R-Cl + SO2 + HCl.
Example: C2H5OH + SOCl2 → C2H5Cl + SO2 + HCl.
Thionyl chloride method is preferred because the by-products SO2 and HCl are gases and escape from the reaction mixture.
This gives a relatively pure alkyl chloride.
The preparation of alkyl chlorides from alcohols using SOCl2 is called Darzens method.

notes.heading: Preparation of Haloalkanes from Hydrocarbons
notes.text: Haloalkanes can also be prepared from alkanes and alkenes through halogenation and addition reactions.
points: Alkanes react with chlorine or bromine in the presence of sunlight or heat by free radical halogenation.
General reaction: R-H + X2 → R-X + HX in the presence of hv.
Example: CH4 + Cl2 → CH3Cl + HCl in the presence of hv.
Further substitution can occur: CH3Cl + Cl2 → CH2Cl2 + HCl.
Further reaction: CH2Cl2 + Cl2 → CHCl3 + HCl.
Further reaction: CHCl3 + Cl2 → CCl4 + HCl.
Free radical halogenation of alkanes often gives a mixture of products.
Alkenes react with hydrogen halides to give haloalkanes.
General reaction: C=C + HX → haloalkane.
Example: CH2=CH2 + HBr → CH3CH2Br.
In unsymmetrical alkenes, addition of HX follows Markovnikov's rule.
Markovnikov's rule states that hydrogen goes to the carbon already having more hydrogen atoms.
Example: CH3CH=CH2 + HBr → CH3CHBrCH3. Propene gives 2-bromopropane as the major product.
In the presence of peroxide, HBr adds according to anti-Markovnikov rule.
Example: CH3CH=CH2 + HBr in the presence of peroxide gives CH3CH2CH2Br.
Anti-Markovnikov addition in presence of peroxide is also called peroxide effect or Kharasch effect.
Peroxide effect is shown only by HBr, not by HCl or HI.

notes.heading: Preparation of Haloalkanes by Halogen Exchange Reactions
notes.text: Halogen exchange reactions are used to replace one halogen atom in a haloalkane by another halogen atom.
points: In halogen exchange reactions, one halogen atom is replaced by another halogen atom.
Finkelstein reaction is used to prepare alkyl iodides from alkyl chlorides or alkyl bromides.
General Finkelstein reaction: R-Cl + NaI → R-I + NaCl.
Example: CH3CH2Cl + NaI → CH3CH2I + NaCl.
Finkelstein reaction is carried out in dry acetone.
NaCl is insoluble in acetone, so it precipitates out and drives the reaction forward.
Swarts reaction is used to prepare fluoroalkanes.
In Swarts reaction, alkyl chlorides or bromides react with metallic fluorides.
Common reagents for Swarts reaction include AgF, Hg2F2, CoF2 and SbF3.
General Swarts reaction: R-Cl + AgF → R-F + AgCl.
Example: CH3Br + AgF → CH3F + AgBr.

notes.heading: Preparation of Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloarenes can be prepared by electrophilic substitution of arenes and from diazonium salts.
points: Haloarenes are compounds in which halogen is directly attached to an aromatic ring.
Benzene reacts with chlorine or bromine in the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst to give haloarenes.
Catalysts used for halogenation of benzene include FeCl3, FeBr3 and AlCl3.
General reaction: C6H6 + X2 → C6H5X + HX in the presence of FeX3.
Chlorination of benzene: C6H6 + Cl2 → C6H5Cl + HCl in the presence of FeCl3.
Bromination of benzene: C6H6 + Br2 → C6H5Br + HBr in the presence of FeBr3.
Benzene is very stable due to resonance, so a catalyst is needed to generate a strong electrophile.
Example of electrophile formation: Cl2 + FeCl3 → Cl+ + FeCl4-.
Haloarenes can be prepared from diazonium salts.
Diazonium salts are prepared from aniline by diazotisation.
Aniline reacts with sodium nitrite and hydrochloric acid at 273-278 K to form benzene diazonium chloride.
Diazotisation reaction: C6H5NH2 + NaNO2 + 2HCl → C6H5N2+Cl- + NaCl + 2H2O.
Sandmeyer reaction is used to prepare chlorobenzene and bromobenzene from benzene diazonium chloride.
Preparation of chlorobenzene: C6H5N2+Cl- → C6H5Cl + N2 using CuCl/HCl.
Preparation of bromobenzene: C6H5N2+Cl- → C6H5Br + N2 using CuBr/HBr.
Gattermann reaction uses copper powder with HCl or HBr to prepare chlorobenzene or bromobenzene.
Gattermann chlorination: C6H5N2+Cl- → C6H5Cl + N2 using Cu/HCl.
Gattermann bromination: C6H5N2+Cl- → C6H5Br + N2 using Cu/HBr.
Iodobenzene is prepared by treating benzene diazonium chloride with potassium iodide.
Reaction: C6H5N2+Cl- + KI → C6H5I + KCl + N2.
Fluorobenzene is prepared by Balz-Schiemann reaction.
In Balz-Schiemann reaction, benzene diazonium fluoroborate on heating gives fluorobenzene.
Reaction: C6H5N2+BF4- → C6H5F + BF3 + N2 on heating.
Diazonium salt method is useful because direct fluorination and iodination of benzene are difficult.

notes.heading: Physical Properties of Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: Physical properties of haloalkanes and haloarenes include physical state, boiling point, melting point, density and solubility.
points: Haloalkanes and haloarenes may be gases, liquids or solids depending on molecular mass.
Lower haloalkanes such as CH3Cl, CH3Br and C2H5Cl are generally gases at room temperature.
Higher haloalkanes and haloarenes are mostly liquids or solids.
Chlorobenzene, C6H5Cl, is a liquid.
Haloalkanes and haloarenes have higher boiling points than hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass.
The higher boiling points are due to the polar C-X bond and stronger intermolecular forces.
For the same alkyl group, boiling point increases with increase in size and mass of halogen.
Boiling point order: R-F < R-Cl < R-Br < R-I.
Example: CH3F < CH3Cl < CH3Br < CH3I.
Iodoalkanes have stronger van der Waals forces because iodine has larger size and higher molecular mass.
Boiling point increases with increase in carbon chain length.
Example: CH3Cl < C2H5Cl < C3H7Cl < C4H9Cl.
For isomeric haloalkanes, boiling point decreases with increase in branching.
Example: 1-chlorobutane has higher boiling point than 2-chloro-2-methylpropane.
Branching decreases surface area and therefore decreases intermolecular attraction.
Melting point depends on how well molecules pack in the crystal lattice.
Symmetrical molecules generally have higher melting points.
p-dichlorobenzene has higher melting point than o-dichlorobenzene and m-dichlorobenzene.
The para-isomer is more symmetrical and packs better in the crystal lattice.
Haloalkanes and haloarenes containing heavier halogens are generally denser than water.
Density order: R-Cl < R-Br < R-I.
Haloalkanes and haloarenes are generally insoluble or very slightly soluble in water.
They cannot form strong hydrogen bonds with water and cannot easily break the hydrogen bonding network of water.
Haloalkanes and haloarenes are soluble in organic solvents such as ether, benzene and alcohol.

notes.heading: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions of Haloalkanes
notes.text: Haloalkanes mainly undergo nucleophilic substitution reactions because the carbon attached to halogen has partial positive charge.
points: Nucleophilic substitution means replacement of halogen by a nucleophile.
General reaction: R-X + Nu- → R-Nu + X-.
In haloalkanes, the C-X bond is polar and the carbon atom has partial positive charge.
The partially positive carbon atom is attacked by electron-rich nucleophiles.
Common nucleophiles include OH-, CN-, NH3, RO-, I- and SH-.
Example: CH3Br + OH- → CH3OH + Br-.
Haloalkanes react with aqueous KOH to give alcohols.
Reaction: R-X + KOH(aq) → R-OH + KX.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KOH(aq) → CH3CH2OH + KBr.
Haloalkanes react with sodium alkoxides to give ethers.
Reaction: R-X + R'O-Na+ → R-O-R' + NaX.
Example: CH3Br + C2H5ONa → CH3OC2H5 + NaBr.
Formation of ethers from haloalkanes and sodium alkoxides is called Williamson ether synthesis.
Haloalkanes react with alcoholic KCN to form alkyl cyanides or nitriles.
Reaction: R-X + KCN → R-CN + KX.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KCN → CH3CH2CN + KBr.
Nitrile formation increases the carbon chain by one carbon atom.
Haloalkanes react with AgCN to form alkyl isocyanides.
Reaction: R-X + AgCN → R-NC + AgX.
Example: CH3Br + AgCN → CH3NC + AgBr.
KCN gives alkyl cyanide, R-CN, because KCN is ionic and CN- attacks through carbon.
AgCN gives alkyl isocyanide, R-NC, because AgCN is more covalent and attack occurs through nitrogen.
Haloalkanes react with ammonia to form amines.
Reaction: R-X + NH3 → R-NH2 + HX.
Example: C2H5Br + NH3 → C2H5NH2 + HBr.
Haloalkanes react with silver nitrite to form nitroalkanes.
Reaction: R-X + AgNO2 → R-NO2 + AgX.
Example: CH3Br + AgNO2 → CH3NO2 + AgBr.
Haloalkanes react with potassium nitrite to form alkyl nitrites.
Reaction: R-X + KNO2 → R-ONO + KX.
Example: CH3Br + KNO2 → CH3ONO + KBr.
KNO2 gives alkyl nitrite, R-ONO, while AgNO2 gives nitroalkane, R-NO2.
Leaving group ability order: I- > Br- > Cl- > F-.
Reactivity order of haloalkanes in nucleophilic substitution: RI > RBr > RCl > RF.

notes.heading: SN1 and SN2 Mechanisms
notes.text: Nucleophilic substitution in haloalkanes takes place mainly by SN1 and SN2 mechanisms.
points: SN1 means substitution nucleophilic unimolecular.
SN2 means substitution nucleophilic bimolecular.
SN2 reaction takes place in one step.
In SN2 reaction, the nucleophile attacks the carbon at the same time as the halogen leaves.
SN2 involves backside attack by the nucleophile.
SN2 mechanism can be represented as Nu- + R-X → [Nu---R---X] → R-Nu + X-.
A transition state is formed in SN2 reaction.
In the transition state, the bond between carbon and nucleophile is partly formed and the bond between carbon and halogen is partly broken.
Rate law of SN2 reaction: Rate = k[R-X][Nu-].
SN2 is called bimolecular because two species are involved in the rate-determining step.
Reactivity order for SN2: CH3X > 1° > 2° > 3°.
SN2 is favoured by less steric hindrance.
Tertiary haloalkanes usually do not undergo SN2 easily because bulky groups block backside attack.
SN2 reaction gives inversion of configuration.
Inversion of configuration in SN2 is called Walden inversion.
SN1 reaction takes place in two steps.
In the first step of SN1, the C-X bond breaks slowly to form a carbocation: R-X → R+ + X-.
The first step is the slow step and the rate-determining step.
In the second step of SN1, the nucleophile attacks the carbocation: R+ + Nu- → R-Nu.
Example: (CH3)3CBr + OH- → (CH3)3COH + Br-.
Rate law of SN1 reaction: Rate = k[R-X].
SN1 is called unimolecular because the rate depends only on haloalkane concentration.
Reactivity order for SN1: 3° > 2° > 1° > CH3X.
SN1 depends on carbocation stability.
Carbocation stability order: 3° > 2° > 1° > CH3+.
Tertiary haloalkanes undergo SN1 easily because tertiary carbocations are more stable.
In SN1, the carbocation intermediate is planar.
Because the carbocation is planar, the nucleophile can attack from either side.
SN1 generally gives racemisation.
Racemisation means formation of a mixture of two optical isomers, d-form and l-form.
SN1 is a two-step reaction, forms a carbocation, follows Rate = k[R-X] and gives racemisation.
SN2 is a one-step reaction, does not form a carbocation, follows Rate = k[R-X][Nu-] and gives inversion of configuration.

notes.heading: Elimination Reactions of Haloalkanes
notes.text: Haloalkanes undergo elimination reactions with alcoholic KOH to form alkenes by removal of hydrogen halide.
points: Elimination means removal of atoms or groups from a molecule.
In haloalkanes, one hydrogen atom and one halogen atom are removed from adjacent carbon atoms.
General reaction: R-CH2-CH2-X → R-CH=CH2 + HX.
Haloalkane changes into alkene during elimination.
The reagent used for elimination is alcoholic KOH with heat.
General condition: R-X in alcoholic KOH gives alkene.
Aqueous KOH gives alcohol by substitution, while alcoholic KOH gives alkene by elimination.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KOH(alc.) → CH2=CH2 + KBr + H2O.
Removal of hydrogen halide from haloalkane is called dehydrohalogenation.
The carbon attached to halogen is called alpha carbon.
The carbon next to alpha carbon is called beta carbon.
In beta elimination, halogen is removed from alpha carbon and hydrogen is removed from beta carbon.
Saytzeff's rule is used when more than one alkene can be formed.
Saytzeff's rule states that in dehydrohalogenation, the major product is the alkene having more alkyl groups attached to the double-bonded carbon atoms.
The more substituted alkene is the major product.
Example: 2-bromobutane gives but-1-ene and but-2-ene with alcoholic KOH.
But-2-ene is the major product because it is more substituted and more stable.
Ease of elimination in haloalkanes is generally 3° > 2° > 1°.
Substitution and elimination compete depending on reagent and conditions.
KOH(aq) gives alcohol, while KOH(alc.) with heat gives alkene.

notes.heading: Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions of Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloarenes are less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than haloalkanes, but they can react under harsh conditions or when electron-withdrawing groups are present.
points: Haloarenes are compounds in which halogen is directly attached to an aromatic ring.
Example: C6H5Cl is chlorobenzene.
Haloarenes are less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than haloalkanes.
Chlorobenzene does not easily react with nucleophiles such as OH-, NH2- and CN-.
Reason 1: In chlorobenzene, the lone pair of chlorine participates in resonance with the benzene ring.
Due to resonance, the C-Cl bond gets partial double bond character and becomes shorter and stronger.
Reason 2: In haloarenes, halogen is attached to an sp2 hybridised carbon.
An sp2 carbon has more s-character than sp3 carbon, so the C-X bond in haloarenes is stronger.
Reason 3: Phenyl carbocation is highly unstable, so haloarenes do not easily undergo SN1 reactions.
Reason 4: The electron-rich benzene ring repels nucleophiles.
Chlorobenzene can react with sodium hydroxide under harsh conditions to form phenol.
Reaction: C6H5Cl + NaOH → C6H5ONa + NaCl at 623 K and 300 atm.
Acidification: C6H5ONa + HCl → C6H5OH + NaCl.
Electron-withdrawing groups such as -NO2 at ortho or para positions make nucleophilic substitution easier.
-NO2 withdraws electrons from the benzene ring, making the ring more positive and helping nucleophilic attack.
-NO2 also stabilises the intermediate formed during nucleophilic substitution.
Ortho and para nitro groups are more effective than meta nitro groups.
Reactivity order: p-nitrochlorobenzene > m-nitrochlorobenzene > chlorobenzene.

notes.heading: Electrophilic Substitution Reactions of Haloarenes
notes.text: Haloarenes undergo electrophilic substitution reactions. Halogens are deactivating groups but ortho-para directing groups.
points: Haloarenes undergo electrophilic substitution reactions like benzene.
Common electrophilic substitution reactions of haloarenes include halogenation, nitration, sulphonation, Friedel-Crafts alkylation and Friedel-Crafts acylation.
Halogens are deactivating groups because they withdraw electrons from the benzene ring by -I effect.
Due to -I effect, the benzene ring becomes less reactive than benzene towards electrophilic substitution.
Halogens are ortho-para directing groups because they donate lone pair electrons to the benzene ring by resonance.
Due to resonance, electron density increases at ortho and para positions.
Therefore, incoming electrophiles mainly attack ortho and para positions.
Nitration of chlorobenzene: C6H5Cl + HNO3 in the presence of concentrated H2SO4 gives o-chloronitrobenzene and p-chloronitrobenzene.
Para product is generally more because it has less steric hindrance.
Halogenation of chlorobenzene: C6H5Cl + Cl2 in the presence of FeCl3 gives o-dichlorobenzene and p-dichlorobenzene.
Sulphonation of chlorobenzene with concentrated H2SO4 gives o-chlorobenzenesulphonic acid and p-chlorobenzenesulphonic acid.
Friedel-Crafts alkylation: C6H5Cl + CH3Cl in the presence of AlCl3 gives o-chlorotoluene and p-chlorotoluene.
Friedel-Crafts acylation: C6H5Cl + CH3COCl in the presence of AlCl3 gives o-chloroacetophenone and p-chloroacetophenone.

notes.heading: Polyhalogen Compounds
notes.text: Polyhalogen compounds are organic compounds containing more than one halogen atom. Important examples are dichloromethane, chloroform, iodoform, carbon tetrachloride, freons and DDT.
points: Polyhalogen compounds contain more than one halogen atom.
Examples of polyhalogen compounds include CH2Cl2, CHCl3 and CCl4.
Dichloromethane has formula CH2Cl2.
Common name of CH2Cl2 is methylene chloride.
IUPAC name of CH2Cl2 is dichloromethane.
Dichloromethane is used as a solvent, paint remover, aerosol propellant and metal cleaning agent.
Dichloromethane can affect the nervous system, liver and lungs.
High exposure to dichloromethane may cause dizziness, nausea and unconsciousness.
Chloroform has formula CHCl3.
IUPAC name of chloroform is trichloromethane.
Chloroform was earlier used as an anaesthetic, but it is no longer used for this purpose because it is toxic.
Chloroform is also used as a laboratory solvent.
Chloroform slowly oxidises in air and sunlight to form poisonous phosgene gas.
Oxidation reaction: 2CHCl3 + O2 → 2COCl2 + 2HCl.
COCl2 is phosgene and is highly poisonous.
Chloroform is stored in dark coloured bottles.
A small amount of ethanol is added to chloroform because ethanol reacts with phosgene and converts it into a harmless compound.
Iodoform has formula CHI3.
IUPAC name of iodoform is triiodomethane.
Iodoform is a yellow crystalline solid with a characteristic unpleasant smell.
Iodoform was earlier used as an antiseptic, but its antiseptic property is due to release of iodine.
Carbon tetrachloride has formula CCl4.
IUPAC name of carbon tetrachloride is tetrachloromethane.
Carbon tetrachloride was used as a cleaning solvent, fire extinguisher and solvent for oils and fats.
Carbon tetrachloride is highly toxic and can damage the liver, kidneys and nervous system.
Carbon tetrachloride also contributes to ozone layer depletion.
Freons are chlorofluorocarbon compounds containing carbon, chlorine and fluorine.
Example: CCl2F2 is Freon-12.
IUPAC name of Freon-12 is dichlorodifluoromethane.
Freons were used as refrigerants, air conditioner coolants and aerosol propellants.
Freons damage the ozone layer by releasing chlorine free radicals in the upper atmosphere.
Ozone destruction reactions: Cl• + O3 → ClO• + O2 and ClO• + O → Cl• + O2.
Overall reaction for ozone destruction: O3 + O → 2O2.
The ozone layer protects Earth from harmful ultraviolet radiation.
Ozone layer depletion increases the risk of skin cancer, eye damage and harm to plants and animals.
DDT stands for dichloro diphenyl trichloroethane.
DDT contains two benzene rings, chlorine atoms and a trichloroethane group.
DDT was used as an insecticide and was effective against mosquitoes and other insects.
DDT was used to control diseases such as malaria and typhus.
DDT is non-biodegradable and remains in soil, water and living organisms for a long time.
Increase in concentration of DDT along the food chain is called biomagnification.
Example of biomagnification: water → small organisms → fish → birds.
DDT affects calcium metabolism in birds, causing thin eggshells that break easily and reduce bird population.

notes.heading: Important Conversions from Haloalkanes and Haloarenes
notes.text: Conversions are important for examinations because they test the correct choice of reagents and reaction conditions.
points: Haloalkane to alcohol: R-X + KOH(aq) → R-OH + KX.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KOH(aq) → CH3CH2OH + KBr.
Haloalkane to alkene: R-X + KOH(alc.) → alkene + KX + H2O.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KOH(alc.) → CH2=CH2 + KBr + H2O.
Haloalkane to ether: R-X + R'O-Na+ → R-O-R' + NaX.
Example: CH3Br + C2H5ONa → CH3OC2H5 + NaBr.
Haloalkane to nitrile: R-X + KCN → R-CN + KX.
Example: CH3CH2Br + KCN → CH3CH2CN + KBr.
Haloalkane to isocyanide: R-X + AgCN → R-NC + AgX.
Example: CH3Br + AgCN → CH3NC + AgBr.
Haloalkane to amine: R-X + NH3 → R-NH2 + HX.
Example: C2H5Br + NH3 → C2H5NH2 + HBr.
Haloalkane to nitroalkane: R-X + AgNO2 → R-NO2 + AgX.
Example: CH3Br + AgNO2 → CH3NO2 + AgBr.
Haloalkane to alkyl nitrite: R-X + KNO2 → R-ONO + KX.
Example: CH3Br + KNO2 → CH3ONO + KBr.
Alkyl chloride or bromide to alkyl iodide is done by Finkelstein reaction.
Finkelstein reaction: R-Cl + NaI in dry acetone → R-I + NaCl.
Example: CH3CH2Cl + NaI → CH3CH2I + NaCl.
Alkyl chloride or bromide to alkyl fluoride is done by Swarts reaction.
Swarts reaction: R-Cl + AgF → R-F + AgCl.
Example: CH3CH2Cl + AgF → CH3CH2F + AgCl.
Chlorobenzene to phenol: C6H5Cl + NaOH at 623 K and 300 atm gives C6H5ONa, followed by acidification to C6H5OH.
Aniline to chlorobenzene: first form benzene diazonium chloride using NaNO2/HCl at 273-278 K, then treat with CuCl/HCl.
Aniline to bromobenzene: first form benzene diazonium chloride using NaNO2/HCl at 273-278 K, then treat with CuBr/HBr.
Aniline to iodobenzene: first form benzene diazonium chloride, then treat with KI.
Aniline to fluorobenzene is done by Balz-Schiemann reaction through benzene diazonium fluoroborate.
Shortcut: KOH(aq) gives alcohol.
Shortcut: KOH(alc.) gives alkene.
Shortcut: NaOR gives ether.
Shortcut: KCN gives nitrile.
Shortcut: AgCN gives isocyanide.
Shortcut: AgNO2 gives nitroalkane.
Shortcut: KNO2 gives alkyl nitrite.
Shortcut: NaI in dry acetone gives alkyl iodide.
Shortcut: AgF gives alkyl fluoride.

notes.heading: Important Exam-Oriented Points
notes.text: These points collect important explanations and distinctions repeatedly used in NCERT-based questions from this chapter.
points: Haloalkanes are more reactive than haloarenes because the C-X bond in haloalkanes is weaker and breaks more easily.
Chlorobenzene is less reactive than chloroethane because the C-Cl bond in chlorobenzene has partial double bond character due to resonance.
Chlorobenzene gives ortho and para products in electrophilic substitution because chlorine donates lone pair electrons by resonance and increases electron density at ortho and para positions.
Chlorine is deactivating due to -I effect but ortho-para directing due to +R effect.
p-dichlorobenzene has higher melting point than ortho and meta isomers because it is more symmetrical and packs better in the crystal lattice.
Haloalkanes are insoluble in water because they cannot form strong hydrogen bonds with water and cannot break the hydrogen bonding network of water.
Ethanol is added to chloroform to convert poisonous phosgene into a harmless compound.
KCN gives R-CN while AgCN gives R-NC.
KNO2 gives R-ONO while AgNO2 gives R-NO2.
Thionyl chloride is preferred for preparing alkyl chlorides from alcohols because gaseous by-products SO2 and HCl escape, giving pure alkyl chloride.
Finkelstein reaction converts alkyl chlorides or bromides into alkyl iodides using NaI in dry acetone.
Swarts reaction prepares alkyl fluorides using metallic fluorides such as AgF.
Sandmeyer reaction prepares chlorobenzene and bromobenzene from benzene diazonium chloride using CuCl/HCl or CuBr/HBr.
SN2 reaction gives inversion of configuration due to backside attack.
SN1 reaction gives racemisation because the planar carbocation can be attacked from either side.
Saytzeff's rule states that the more substituted alkene is the major product in elimination reactions.
DDT causes environmental problems because it is non-biodegradable and undergoes biomagnification.
Freons are harmful because they release chlorine radicals that destroy ozone in the upper atmosphere.
